prior_learning: Students will benefit from visual sequences and patterns initially as well as physical sequences eg., match sticks, counters.
Students will have seen algebraic puzzles before using pictures rather than variables and these are a good starting point.
Link rules with  number to rules with algebra.
It is important that teachers assess retention before starting the topic so that they can build on prior learning.
Students will have been exposed to sequences as function machines and these are a useful starting point and support for individuals where necessary.
With index laws it is important to teach it with understanding rather than just a rule – link it into prior knowledge of cancellation of terms.
Be explicit that equations and formulas are really the same things with different names.
common_misconceptions: Avoid using a for apples and b for bananas in your vocabulary so that students understand a ≠ 1 apple.  Use ‘the number of’ in your language eg a = a number of apples.
Be specific with mathematical algebraic conventions eg 2t means 2 x t
Be specific with language such as the difference between an expression and an equation.
Be specific with equations, formulas, formulae, functions are all really the same thing
Clarify that BODMAS/BIDMAS is a mathematical convention so that we don’t get different answers
‘Simplify’ in English suggests the answer looks simpler whereas in maths ‘simplify’ means re-write and the answer often doesn’t look simpler
Clarify that sometimes in maths we only ask for something to be simplified, rather than solved.  This means the answer will still have variables in it.
Check calculator does BODMAS/BIDMAS or students will need to use brackets on calculators
With index laws it is important to teach it with understanding rather than just a rule – link it into prior knowledge of cancellation of terms.

learning_outcomes: Understanding algebraic conventions eg., 2a means 2 x a etc
Simplifying algebraic expressions involving addition and subtraction
key_knowledge_components: This lesson is a pre-cursor to “I know how to expand and factorise single and double brackets”

key_vocab: Pattern, generalise, predict, substitute, inverse, term, cancelling out/cancelling down

key_questions: What does 3a mean? Is it 3+ a, 3-a or what?
Can I collect 3a with 4a?
Can I collect 3a with 3a2? Why not?
What about 3a and 3ab – do we collect them together?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Avoid using a for apples and b for bananas in your vocabulary so that students understand a ≠ 1 apple.  Use ‘the number of’ in your language eg a = a number of apples. Students confuse x2  with 2x Be specific with mathematical algebraic conventions eg 2t means 2 x t Be specific with language such as the difference between an expression and an equation Link rules with number into rules with algebra eg   cancels to 2,  cancels to 2y etc

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons.
notes.deepening_knowledge_summary: Use examples with lots of negatives to recap directed number – remind students that rules for algebra are the same as rules for number. Use examples where BODMAS/BIDMAS is involved so that students extend their knowledge of number Week/ Lesson Key Question? Key knowledge components & Lesson Outcomes What do students need to know & what should they be able to do?

Simplifying algebraic expressions involving multiplication/division

key_vocab: Pattern, generalise, predict, substitute, inverse, term, cancelling out/cancelling down

key_questions: What is   equal to?
What is  equal to?
Can you simplify it further?  Are there any ‘like terms’ that you can collect together?
Can you collect 2y with 2yz? Why not?
Can you collect 2y with 2y2 ? Why not?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Avoid using a for apples and b for bananas in your vocabulary so that students understand a ≠ 1 apple.  Use ‘the number of’ in your language eg a = a number of apples. Students confuse x2  with 2x Be specific with mathematical algebraic conventions eg 2t means 2 x t Be specific with language such as the difference between an expression and an equation Link rules with number into rules with algebra eg   cancels to 2,  cancels to 2y etc

learning_outcomes: Multiplying a single digit over a bracket
Multiplying a single digit over a bracket and then collecting like terms
key_knowledge_components: I know how to expand and factorise single and double brackets
I can expand and factorise single and double brackets

key_vocab: Expand, multiply out brackets, collect like terms, brackets, indices, powers, grid method

key_questions: How do we use the grid method with numbers? Can we use it the same way with algebra? How can we partition 26 (20 and 6)? How can we partition x+5 (x and 5)?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Revise previous lesson in starter with activity such as: Use the grid method to multiply a 2 digit number by a single digit first eg 23 x 6. Then show how we can also use the grid method to multiply 4(x+5) x 5 4 4x

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Use grid method with number before linking it into algebra. Don’t remove the grid with algebra too early. Have copies of ready made grids available.
notes.deepening_knowledge_summary: Involve directed number and some simple fractions and decimals eg, 4(x + ) Week/ Lesson Key Question? Key knowledge components & Lesson Outcomes What do students need to know & what should they be able to do? Pedagogy & Assessment– How do we teach and assess this at Coal Clough Academy? Strategies to support students knowing more & remembering more

learning_outcomes: Multiplying double brackets and collecting like terms
key_knowledge_components: I know how to expand and factorise single and double brackets
I can expand and factorise single and double brackets

key_vocab: Multiply, brackets, collect like terms, quadratic, equation, brackets, indices, powers, grid method

key_questions: How did we multiply out a single bracket?
What is x times x? Is it 2x?
How did we do this when we did negative numbers?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start with expansion of a bracket with a single term from pervious lesson eg 4(x + 5) using grid method and then extend to double brackets eg (x+3)(x+ 4) x +3 x X2 3x

Don’t take the grid away from students too early (have copies of ready made grids available)

teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Use some examples of the grid method to multiply where one factor is missing but you have the expanded answer   so students can see that factorizing is the inverse of expanding. x 5 ? 4x 20

learning_outcomes: Solving linear equations using inverses
key_knowledge_components: I know mathematical conventions for solving equations containing fractions, brackets and variables on both sides.
I can solve equations containing fractions, brackets and variables on both sides.

key_vocab: Solve, evaluate, calculate, inverse, simplify, expand, factorise, collect like terms

key_questions: What steps do I need to take to solve this?
What should I do first? Why?
If I have a scale and I take 3 off one side what will happen to the scale? How can I make it balance again?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Avoid using a for apples and b for bananas in your vocabulary so that students understand a ≠ 1 apple.  Use ‘the number of’ in your language eg a = a number of apples. Students confuse x2  with 2x Be specific with mathematical algebraic conventions eg 2t means 2 x t Be specific with language such as the difference between an expression and an equation Use scales to show how to keep it equal whatever you do to one side you need to do to the other.

learning_outcomes: Finding position to term rules
Writing position to term rules algebraically
Using position to term rules to predict the nth term
key_knowledge_components: I know how to find a position to term rule (nth term) to find terms and positions in a sequence.
I can use a position to term rule (nth term) to find terms and positions in a sequence.

key_vocab: Pattern, generalise, predict, substitute, inverse, term, sequence

key_questions: What is the 100th term? What is the 1000th term? Why is the term to term rule limited?
What are you doing to the variable first?  What are you doing second?
How can I write this algebraically?
What is the sequence going up/down in each time?
What would the 0th term be? Or what would the term before the first term be?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Starter: start calling out a sequence and have a competition to see who can predict the next term and the term to term rule first – use more difficult example than last lesson Get students to write their own sequences and then swap with a partner to find the next term and the term to term rule. Avoid using a for apples and b for bananas in your vocabulary so that students understand a ≠ 1 apple.  Use ‘the number of’ in your language eg a = a num…

Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Use function machines to support and give students blank function machines to use as they wish.
